title: DNA replication
summary: This lesson details the process of DNA replication, focusing on its semi-conservative nature. We will explore the crucial roles of the enzymes helicase and DNA polymerase in creating two identical DNA molecules from one original, a fundamental process for cell division and inheritance.
learningObjectives: Explain that DNA replication is semi-conservative.
Describe the role of helicase in unwinding the double helix and separating the two strands by breaking hydrogen bonds.
Describe the role of DNA polymerase in synthesising new strands by linking nucleotides together.
Outline the process of DNA replication, including the formation of two new DNA molecules identical to the original.

simpleExplanation.title: Photocopying the Blueprint of Life
simpleExplanation.summary: DNA replication is the cell's way of making an exact copy of its DNA before it divides. This ensures that each new cell receives a complete and identical set of genetic instructions.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a book with two bound halves. To copy it, you first separate the two halves. Then, you use each half as a template to write out a new, matching half, resulting in two complete, identical books. In DNA, the 'old' halves are conserved in the new copies.
steps: The enzyme helicase unwinds the DNA double helix, like unzipping a jacket.
Helicase then breaks the weak hydrogen bonds holding the two strands together, separating them.
The enzyme DNA polymerase moves along each separated strand, adding new, complementary nucleotides.
Two new, identical DNA molecules are formed, each consisting of one original strand and one newly synthesised strand.

flashcards.back: The process by which a double-stranded DNA molecule is copied to produce two identical DNA molecules.
The mechanism of DNA replication in which each new DNA molecule consists of one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesised strand.
The enzyme that unwinds the DNA double helix and separates the two strands by breaking the hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs.
The enzyme that synthesises new DNA strands by adding nucleotides that are complementary to the template strand, in a 5' to 3' direction.
An original DNA strand that is used to synthesise a new, complementary DNA strand during replication.
The rule that dictates the pairing of bases in DNA: Adenine (A) with Thymine (T), and Guanine (G) with Cytosine (C).
The building block of DNA, consisting of a deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base (A, T, C, or G).
The weak chemical bonds that hold the two strands of the DNA double helix together between the complementary base pairs.
The direction in which DNA polymerase synthesises a new DNA strand, adding new nucleotides to the 3' end of the growing chain.
A common error is swapping the roles of helicase and DNA polymerase. Remember: Helicase 'un-helices' (unwinds), while Polymerase 'builds a polymer' (the new strand).

flashcards.front: Where does the energy for adding nucleotides come from?
flashcards.back: The nucleotides added are deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates (dNTPs). The cleavage of two phosphate groups provides the energy to form the phosphodiester bond.

faq.a: It's called 'semi-conservative' because half ('semi') of the original DNA molecule is saved, or conserved, in each of the two new molecules. Each new double helix is a hybrid of one old strand and one new strand.

faq.q: What happens if DNA polymerase makes a mistake?
faq.a: DNA polymerase has a proofreading function. It can 'backtrack' to remove an incorrectly paired nucleotide and replace it with the correct one. While not perfect, this mechanism makes DNA replication incredibly accurate, with an error rate of about one in a billion nucleotides.

faq.q: Where in the cell cycle does DNA replication occur?
faq.a: DNA replication takes place during the S phase (Synthesis phase) of interphase, which is the period of the cell cycle between cell divisions (mitosis).

faq.q: Why does DNA polymerase only work in the 5' to 3' direction?
faq.a: This is due to the chemical structure of the nucleotides and the active site of the enzyme. DNA polymerase can only add a new nucleotide to the hydroxyl (-OH) group found on the 3' carbon of the deoxyribose sugar of the preceding nucleotide. This inherent chemical constraint dictates the direction of synthesis.

sections.content: Before a cell can divide, it must make a complete and faithful copy of its genetic material. This process, known as DNA replication, occurs during the S phase of interphase in the cell cycle. It ensures that both daughter cells receive an identical set of chromosomes and, therefore, the same genetic instructions as the parent cell.

DNA replication is described as 'semi-conservative'. This means that when a new DNA molecule is formed, it consists of one strand from the original molecule and one newly synthesised strand. The original strand acts as a template, dictating the sequence of the new strand through complementary base pairing. This model was confirmed by the elegant experiments of Meselson and Stahl in 1958, which used nitrogen isotopes to track the original and new DNA strands over several generations of bacterial cell division.

The process of DNA replication is orchestrated by a team of highly specific enzymes. For the SL course, you need to know the roles of two main players: helicase and DNA polymerase. These enzymes work together with incredible speed and accuracy to copy the entire genome.

items: **Helicase:** This enzyme's primary function is to unwind the DNA double helix. It moves along the DNA molecule, breaking the hydrogen bonds between the complementary base pairs, effectively 'unzipping' the two strands to expose the bases.

**DNA Polymerase:** This is the 'builder' enzyme. It moves along each of the separated template strands and adds free nucleotides from the nucleoplasm. It always follows the complementary base pairing rule (A with T, C with G) and synthesises the new strand in a 5' to 3' direction.

sections.content: The process begins at specific locations on the DNA called origins of replication. Here, helicase binds and starts to unwind the DNA, creating a 'replication bubble' with two 'replication forks'. As the two strands separate, they become template strands for the synthesis of new DNA.

Free-floating deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates (dNTPs) in the nucleus are used as the building blocks. DNA polymerase binds to the template strand and begins adding these nucleotides one by one. It reads the template strand and adds the corresponding complementary nucleotide to the 3' end of the growing new strand. This process continues until the entire molecule is copied, resulting in two DNA molecules that are genetically identical to each other and to the original molecule.

sections.question: A diagram of a replication fork shows a molecule labelled 'X' separating the two DNA strands. Another molecule, labelled 'Y', is shown adding nucleotides to form a new strand. Identify molecules X and Y and describe their functions.
sections.solution:
X is helicase. [1 mark] Its function is to unwind the DNA double helix and break the hydrogen bonds between the base pairs. [1 mark]

Y is DNA polymerase. [1 mark] Its function is to synthesise a new DNA strand by linking nucleotides together, using the original strand as a template and following complementary base pairing rules. [1 mark]

sections.question: A single DNA molecule undergoes three complete rounds of replication in a medium containing non-radioactive nucleotides. The original DNA molecule was made entirely of a radioactive isotope. After three rounds, what percentage of the DNA molecules will contain any of the original radioactive material?
sections.solution:
The replication is semi-conservative, meaning each new molecule contains one old strand and one new strand.

Original molecule: 1 (contains 2 radioactive strands).

After Round 1: 2 molecules are formed. Both molecules contain one original radioactive strand and one new non-radioactive strand. So, 2 out of 2 molecules (100%) contain radioactivity.

After Round 2: 4 molecules are formed. The 2 radioactive strands from the original molecule are now distributed among 4 molecules. Therefore, 2 of the 4 molecules will contain one radioactive strand each. The other 2 molecules will be entirely non-radioactive. So, 2 out of 4 molecules (50%) contain radioactivity.

After Round 3: 8 molecules are formed. The 2 original radioactive strands are now distributed among 8 molecules. Therefore, 2 of the 8 molecules will contain one radioactive strand each. [1 mark] The other 6 will be entirely non-radioactive.

Percentage of molecules with radioactivity = (2 / 8) * 100% = 25%. [1 mark]

sections.content: In exam questions, always use the term 'semi-conservative' when explaining DNA replication. When describing the roles of enzymes, be precise. State that helicase 'breaks hydrogen bonds' not just 'separates strands'. State that DNA polymerase 'links nucleotides' or 'forms phosphodiester bonds' in a '5\' to 3\' direction'.
